prompts.title: Generate Structured Case Brief for Indian Supreme Court Judgment
prompts.prompt: Act as a senior law professor at NLSIU Bangalore with 15 years experience teaching Constitutional Law and Legal Methods. I need you to prepare a comprehensive case brief for a Supreme Court of India judgment that I will paste below. Here is the context: I am a 2nd year LLB student at GLC Mumbai preparing for tutorials and need a consistent case-briefing format I can reuse for every judgment. Please provide: (1) Case citation in SCC style (e.g., (1973) 4 SCC 225), (2) Bench composition and majority/minority split, (3) Material facts in 150 words max, (4) Issues framed by the Court as numbered questions of law, (5) Arguments of petitioner and respondent separately, (6) Ratio decidendi in 2-3 sentences, (7) Obiter dicta if any, (8) Final holding and directions, (9) Precedents relied on with citations, (10) Significance for Indian constitutional jurisprudence. Format the output as a clean markdown document with headers for each section. Consider these constraints: use SCC/Manupatra citation style, reference specific Articles of the Constitution, distinguish between ratio and obiter clearly, and flag any dissenting opinions. Paste the judgment text or case name here: [INSERT JUDGMENT].

prompts.title: Draft Moot Court Memorial for Petitioner under Article 32
prompts.prompt: Act as a moot court coach at NALSAR Hyderabad with 10 years experience mentoring teams for Jessup, Henry Dunant, and NLS Trilegal moots. I need you to draft a complete memorial on behalf of the Petitioner for a writ petition under Article 32 of the Constitution of India. Here is the context: I am a 3rd year LLB student preparing for an intra-college moot where the fact pattern involves a fundamental rights violation. The facts are: [INSERT MOOT PROPOSITION]. Please provide: (1) Cover page with case title and appearances, (2) Table of Contents, (3) List of Abbreviations, (4) Index of Authorities (cases, statutes, books, articles) in Bluebook 20th edition, (5) Statement of Jurisdiction citing Article 32, (6) Statement of Facts in neutral tone (300 words), (7) Issues Raised as numbered questions, (8) Summary of Arguments (1 page), (9) Arguments Advanced with at least 3 issues each supported by SC precedents like Maneka Gandhi, Puttaswamy, and Vishaka, (10) Prayer. Format the output as a formal moot memorial. Consider these constraints: maximum 25 pages equivalent, pin-point citations required, use Indian SC and HC cases primarily, follow Bar Council of India moot conventions, and maintain footnote discipline throughout.

prompts.title: Explain IPC Section 302 vs 304 Culpable Homicide Distinction
prompts.prompt: Act as a criminal law professor with 20 years experience at Faculty of Law, Delhi University. I need you to create a detailed comparative analysis of Section 302 (murder) versus Section 304 Part I and Part II (culpable homicide not amounting to murder) under the Indian Penal Code, 1860, with cross-references to the corresponding Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023 provisions. Here is the context: I am a 1st year LLB student preparing for my Criminal Law I semester exam and keep confusing the four exceptions in Section 300. Please provide: (1) Plain language definition of each section, (2) Essential ingredients in bullet form, (3) The four exceptions to Section 300 with one landmark case illustrating each (e.g., K.M. Nanavati, Virsa Singh, Reg v. Govinda), (4) A comparison table showing mens rea, actus reus, punishment, and burden of proof, (5) A decision tree flowchart in text form to classify a fact pattern, (6) Five hypothetical MCQs with answers testing the distinction, (7) Corresponding BNS sections and what has changed, (8) Exam writing tips for a 15-mark question. Format the output as structured study notes with tables. Consider these constraints: cite IPC sections precisely, use Indian case names, and reference the 2023 BNS reforms.

prompts.title: Draft Simple Sale Deed under Indian Contract Act 1872
prompts.prompt: Act as a conveyancing lawyer with 18 years experience drafting property documents in Mumbai and Pune. I need you to draft a complete sale deed for an immovable residential property transaction in Maharashtra. Here is the context: I am a 2nd year LLB student at ILS Pune learning contract drafting, and my professor asked me to prepare a sale deed for a 2BHK flat worth Rs. 85 lakh being sold by Rajesh Sharma (seller) to Priya Iyer (buyer) in Kothrud, Pune. The flat is in a registered co-operative housing society. Please provide: (1) Proper recital with parties, description, and consideration, (2) Schedule of property with survey number, area, boundaries, (3) Covenants of the seller regarding marketable title, (4) Indemnity clauses, (5) Payment terms including earnest money and balance, (6) Possession and handover clause, (7) Stamp duty and registration responsibility, (8) Society NOC reference, (9) Witness attestation block, (10) Annexures list. Format the output as a formal deed with numbered clauses. Consider these constraints: comply with the Transfer of Property Act 1882, Indian Stamp Act, Registration Act 1908, Maharashtra stamp duty rates, Section 54 of TPA, and standard Section 10 Indian Contract Act requirements for valid contract.

prompts.title: Analyze Kesavananda Bharati Basic Structure Doctrine
prompts.prompt: Act as a constitutional law scholar and former judicial clerk at the Supreme Court of India with 12 years experience. I need you to produce an in-depth analytical note on Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala (1973) 4 SCC 225 and the Basic Structure Doctrine. Here is the context: I am a 3rd year LLB student writing a term paper on constitutional amendments for my Constitutional Law II course at NLU Delhi. Please provide: (1) Historical background from Shankari Prasad through Golaknath to Kesavananda, (2) The 13-judge bench composition and the 7:6 split with key opinions of Sikri CJI, Khanna J., and Ray J., (3) The precise ratio as distilled by the Court in subsequent cases, (4) List of basic features identified across cases (secularism, federalism, judicial review, rule of law, free and fair elections), (5) Application of the doctrine in Indira Nehru Gandhi, Minerva Mills, Waman Rao, S.R. Bommai, IR Coelho, (6) Critiques of the doctrine from Indian and comparative perspectives, (7) Its relevance to recent amendments like NJAC and 103rd amendment, (8) A 300-word exam-ready summary. Format the output as an academic research note with footnotes. Consider these constraints: use SCC citations, reference specific paragraph numbers where possible, and maintain neutral academic tone.

prompts.title: Prepare Oral Round Rebuttals for Moot Court Competition
prompts.prompt: Act as a moot court oralist coach with 10 years experience training National Law University teams for international moots. I need you to prepare a complete rebuttal strategy and anticipated questions bank for a moot oral round. Here is the context: I am speaker 1 for the Respondent in a constitutional law moot at Symbiosis Pune, and the issue is whether a state law violates Article 19(1)(a) freedom of speech. The Petitioner's main arguments are: [INSERT 3 ARGUMENTS]. Please provide: (1) 15 likely judge questions ranked by difficulty with 60-word model answers, (2) Three counter-arguments to each petitioner point with supporting case law, (3) A 2-minute opening statement and a 90-second rebuttal script, (4) Key reasonable restrictions jurisprudence under Article 19(2) with cases like Shreya Singhal, Kedar Nath Singh, Romesh Thappar, (5) Pin-point page references from the compendium, (6) Phrases to buy time gracefully, (7) Etiquette for addressing the bench (May it please Your Lordships format), (8) Red-flag traps to avoid. Format the output as a speaker prep sheet. Consider these constraints: use BCI moot etiquette, Indian SC precedents, and realistic 15-minute oral round time budgeting.

prompts.title: Solve Criminal Law Hypothetical under IPC Sections 299-304
prompts.prompt: Act as a criminal trial court judge with 22 years experience on the bench in Delhi. I need you to solve a detailed criminal law hypothetical problem and write a reasoned answer as if preparing a model answer for an LLB exam. Here is the context: Amit, a Bengaluru resident, discovers his wife Kavya in a compromising position with his friend Rohan. In a fit of rage, he picks up a cricket bat and strikes Rohan on the head multiple times. Rohan dies 4 days later in NIMHANS hospital from head injuries. Amit surrenders at Indiranagar police station the next morning. Please provide: (1) Identification of all relevant IPC/BNS sections that could apply, (2) Step-by-step application of Section 299 and Section 300 definitions, (3) Analysis of whether Exception 1 (grave and sudden provocation) applies with the K.M. Nanavati test, (4) Alternate charging scenarios (Section 302 vs 304 Part I vs 304 Part II), (5) Relevant mens rea analysis, (6) Defenses Amit could raise, (7) Prosecution burden and standard of proof, (8) Likely verdict with reasoning, (9) Sentencing considerations under CrPC Section 354(3). Format the output as a structured legal opinion with headings. Consider these constraints: apply Indian law strictly, cite landmark cases, and reach a defensible conclusion.

prompts.title: Research Legal Position on Live-In Relationships in India
prompts.prompt: Act as a legal researcher at the Indian Law Institute, New Delhi with 8 years experience producing doctrinal research papers. I need you to prepare a comprehensive legal research note on the status of live-in relationships under Indian law. Here is the context: I am a 4th year LLB student working on my seminar paper on evolving family law in India and need a foundation document I can build from. Please provide: (1) Definition and judicial recognition starting from S. Khushboo v. Kanniammal, (2) Key Supreme Court cases: Indra Sarma, D. Velusamy, Lata Singh, Madan Mohan Singh, (3) Rights available under Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act 2005 to female live-in partners, (4) Status of children born out of such relationships under Hindu Marriage Act and succession laws, (5) Maintenance rights under Section 125 CrPC (now BNSS), (6) Presumption of marriage principle, (7) Current gaps and Law Commission recommendations, (8) Comparative reference to other jurisdictions in 100 words, (9) Bibliography with SCC Online and Manupatra citations, (10) Three research questions for further study. Format the output as an academic research memo with footnotes. Consider these constraints: cite only Indian authorities, use SCC pagination, and note unresolved constitutional issues post-Puttaswamy.

prompts.title: Draft Non-Disclosure Agreement for Indian Startup
prompts.prompt: Act as a corporate lawyer with 12 years experience at a tier-1 Indian law firm advising startups in Bengaluru and Gurgaon. I need you to draft a mutual non-disclosure agreement (NDA) that complies with the Indian Contract Act 1872. Here is the context: I am a 3rd year LLB student helping my sibling who runs a SaaS startup in Koramangala that is about to share its product roadmap with a potential investor from Mumbai. The NDA must be governed by Indian law. Please provide: (1) Parties clause with registered address blocks, (2) Effective date and term (2 years), (3) Definition of Confidential Information (broad and narrow carve-outs), (4) Permitted disclosures and need-to-know standard, (5) Obligations of receiving party, (6) Standard exclusions (public domain, independently developed, compelled by law), (7) Return or destruction clause, (8) Remedies including injunctive relief and liquidated damages, (9) Governing law (Indian Contract Act 1872) and Bengaluru jurisdiction, (10) Arbitration under Arbitration and Conciliation Act 1996 seat Bengaluru, (11) Boilerplate (severability, entire agreement, counterparts, notices). Format the output as a clean contract with numbered clauses and signature blocks. Consider these constraints: enforceable under Indian law, avoid US-style legalese, and include Section 27 Contract Act caveats on restraint of trade.

prompts.title: Compare Fundamental Rights and Directive Principles
prompts.prompt: Act as a constitutional law tutor at NLU Jodhpur with 14 years experience coaching CLAT and LLB students. I need you to create detailed study notes comparing Fundamental Rights (Part III) and Directive Principles of State Policy (Part IV) of the Constitution of India. Here is the context: I am a 1st year LLB student preparing for my Constitutional Law I end-semester exam and this topic carries a 20-mark question every year. Please provide: (1) Article-wise mapping of Part III (Articles 12-35) and Part IV (Articles 36-51), (2) Core conceptual differences in a comparison table, (3) Justiciability distinction with Article 37 language, (4) Evolution from Champakam Dorairajan to Minerva Mills to harmonious construction, (5) Key cases: Kesavananda Bharati, Minerva Mills, Unni Krishnan, Olga Tellis, M.C. Mehta, (6) How courts read DPSPs into FRs (e.g., right to livelihood, education, health), (7) The 42nd Amendment and Section 31C, (8) Exam-ready 400-word answer template, (9) 5 past year questions from Delhi University and NLU exams, (10) Memory mnemonics. Format the output as structured revision notes. Consider these constraints: use Indian Constitution article numbers precisely, SC citations, and avoid US constitutional comparisons.

prompts.title: Draft Employment Agreement under Indian Labour Laws
prompts.prompt: Act as an employment law specialist with 15 years experience advising Indian IT and manufacturing companies in Pune and Chennai. I need you to draft a comprehensive employment agreement for a mid-level software engineer. Here is the context: I am a 3rd year LLB student drafting this for my Labour Law assignment. The employee is Arjun Nair joining Zentrix Technologies Pvt Ltd, Hyderabad as Senior Software Engineer at CTC Rs. 18 lakh per annum. Please provide: (1) Offer summary and joining date, (2) Designation, reporting structure, and job location clause, (3) Remuneration breakdown (basic, HRA, PF, gratuity, variable), (4) Working hours as per Telangana Shops and Establishments Act, (5) Leave policy (casual, sick, earned, maternity/paternity), (6) Probation and confirmation, (7) Confidentiality and IP assignment, (8) Non-compete and non-solicit (enforceability note under Section 27 Contract Act), (9) Termination with notice period, (10) Disciplinary and grievance redressal, (11) POSH Act 2013 compliance clause, (12) Governing law and jurisdiction. Format the output as a complete contract. Consider these constraints: comply with Code on Wages 2019, Industrial Relations Code 2020, EPF Act, Payment of Gratuity Act 1972, and Telangana-specific rules.

prompts.title: Analyze Puttaswamy Right to Privacy Judgment
prompts.prompt: Act as a senior advocate practicing in the Supreme Court with 20 years experience in constitutional litigation and 8 years teaching at NLU Delhi. I need you to produce an analytical case brief of Justice K.S. Puttaswamy (Retd.) v. Union of India (2017) 10 SCC 1. Here is the context: I am a 4th year LLB student using this judgment for my dissertation on data protection in India. Please provide: (1) Full citation and 9-judge bench composition, (2) Background from M.P. Sharma and Kharak Singh, (3) The reference question framed, (4) Summary of the six separate but concurring opinions, (5) Ratio: privacy as a fundamental right under Articles 14, 19, and 21, (6) Three-part proportionality test laid down (legality, legitimate aim, proportionality), (7) Application in Aadhaar, Navtej Johar, Joseph Shine, and Anuradha Bhasin, (8) Impact on the Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023, (9) Outstanding questions on horizontal application and surveillance, (10) Critical evaluation in 250 words. Format the output as a detailed case analysis. Consider these constraints: cite paragraph numbers, use SCC citations, and reference post-2017 jurisprudential developments.

prompts.title: CLAT PG Legal Reasoning Practice Questions
prompts.prompt: Act as a CLAT PG preparation coach with 9 years experience at a leading coaching institute in Lucknow with 200+ NLU selections. I need you to create a high-quality legal reasoning practice set. Here is the context: I am a final year LLB student preparing for CLAT PG 2026 to pursue LLM at NLSIU Bangalore and need comprehension-based practice. Please provide: (1) Two passage-based sets of 300-400 words each on Indian Contract Act and Constitutional Law, (2) 10 MCQs per passage totaling 20 questions covering ratio extraction, principle application, and distinguishing facts, (3) Four answer options per question calibrated for difficulty, (4) Detailed explanations showing why each wrong option is wrong, (5) The legal principle tested tagged at the end of each question, (6) Cross-reference to relevant landmark cases, (7) Time budget per question (1.2 minutes), (8) A summary of recurring CLAT PG traps to avoid, (9) Score interpretation (how many correct = NLSIU cutoff). Format the output as an exam practice booklet. Consider these constraints: mirror actual CLAT PG 2024 and 2025 pattern, Indian law only, and medium-hard difficulty.

prompts.title: Draft Legal Notice under Section 138 Negotiable Instruments Act
prompts.prompt: Act as a commercial litigation advocate with 16 years experience handling cheque bounce matters in the Bombay City Civil Court. I need you to draft a legal notice under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act 1881. Here is the context: I am a 3rd year LLB student helping my uncle who runs a textile trading business in Surat. A buyer, Harish Mehta of Mumbai, issued a cheque for Rs. 4,75,000 dated 10 March 2026 which was dishonoured on 14 March 2026 due to insufficient funds. Please provide: (1) Proper sender and addressee blocks, (2) Subject line, (3) Factual narrative referencing the invoice and cheque details, (4) Bank memo of dishonour reference, (5) Legal provisions invoked (Section 138 NI Act, Section 142), (6) Demand for payment within 15 days, (7) Consequences clause (criminal complaint, civil recovery), (8) Reservation of rights, (9) Advocate sign-off with enrolment number, (10) Annexure list. Format the output as a formal advocate notice on letterhead. Consider these constraints: 30-day limitation from dishonour, proper service by registered post AD and email, strict compliance with Section 138 proviso (a), (b), (c), and current NI Act jurisprudence including Dashrath Rupsingh Rathod.

prompts.title: Summarize Vishaka Guidelines and POSH Act 2013
prompts.prompt: Act as a gender justice law professor with 18 years experience and a consultant to the National Commission for Women. I need you to prepare comprehensive study notes on Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan (1997) 6 SCC 241 and its transition to the Sexual Harassment of Women at Workplace (Prevention, Prohibition and Redressal) Act 2013. Here is the context: I am a 2nd year LLB student preparing for my Gender and Law course at Jindal Global Law School. Please provide: (1) Facts of Vishaka and the Bhanwari Devi background, (2) Issues framed and Article 14, 19, 21, and 51(c) reasoning, (3) The Vishaka Guidelines in full (11 points), (4) Use of CEDAW as interpretive aid, (5) How the guidelines evolved into the POSH Act, (6) Key POSH Act provisions: definition, Internal Committee constitution, complaint process, timelines, reliefs, (7) Rules 2013 and employer duties, (8) Landmark cases post-Act: Apparel Export Promotion Council, Medha Kotwal Lele, Ruchika Girhotra-influenced jurisprudence, (9) Compliance checklist for a 100-employee company, (10) Critique and gaps. Format the output as classroom handout notes. Consider these constraints: use SCC citations, Indian statutory language, and current 2024 amendments if any.

prompts.title: Draft Written Statement in Civil Suit under CPC
prompts.prompt: Act as a civil litigation advocate with 14 years practice before the Madras High Court and district courts in Tamil Nadu. I need you to draft a written statement in response to a plaint under the Code of Civil Procedure 1908. Here is the context: I am a 3rd year LLB student preparing drafting assignments at School of Excellence in Law, Chennai. The plaintiff Srinivasan has filed a suit for recovery of Rs. 12 lakh allegedly lent as a friendly loan to my client Defendant Lakshmi Raman in 2023. My client denies the loan and claims the amount was advance payment for a scrapped business venture. Please provide: (1) Cause title with court name and suit number, (2) Preliminary objections (limitation, jurisdiction, cause of action, non-joinder), (3) Para-wise reply corresponding to the plaint (admitted, denied, not admitted), (4) Additional pleas including counter-claim if applicable, (5) Specific denial of loan under Order VIII Rule 3-5 CPC, (6) Prayer to dismiss the suit with costs, (7) Verification clause under Order VI Rule 15, (8) List of documents relied on, (9) Affidavit in support. Format the output as a formal written statement. Consider these constraints: strict CPC compliance, Order VIII Rules 1-10, 30-day filing limit, specific denial rule, and Tamil Nadu court practice.

prompts.title: Explain Doctrine of Frustration under Section 56 Contract Act
prompts.prompt: Act as a contract law professor at Symbiosis Law School Pune with 16 years teaching experience. I need you to prepare detailed study notes on the doctrine of frustration of contract under Section 56 of the Indian Contract Act 1872. Here is the context: I am a 1st year LLB student and my tutorial question asks me to discuss frustration with reference to COVID-19 pandemic disputes in Indian courts. Please provide: (1) Text of Section 56 with plain language explanation, (2) Distinction between initial and subsequent impossibility, (3) Landmark Indian cases: Satyabrata Ghose v. Mugneeram Bangur, Naihati Jute Mills, Energy Watchdog v. CERC, Halliburton v. Vedanta (COVID), (4) English law comparison (Taylor v. Caldwell) and why Indian position is statutory, (5) Categories of frustration: destruction of subject matter, death/incapacity, illegality, change of circumstances, (6) Non-application: mere hardship, self-induced, commercial difficulty, (7) Effect under Section 65 (restitution), (8) Force majeure clause interaction, (9) Application to COVID-19 lease, PPA, and construction disputes, (10) Exam answer framework for a 10-mark question. Format the output as study notes with headings. Consider these constraints: Indian Contract Act primacy, SC citations, and 2020-2023 COVID jurisprudence.

prompts.title: Prepare Bail Application under CrPC Section 439
prompts.prompt: Act as a criminal defence advocate with 17 years experience practicing in Delhi sessions courts and High Court. I need you to draft a regular bail application under Section 439 of the CrPC (corresponding Section 483 BNSS 2023). Here is the context: I am a 4th year LLB student interning at a criminal law chamber in Tis Hazari. The accused Rakesh Gupta is in judicial custody since 15 February 2026 in FIR No. 234/2026 at PS Karol Bagh, charged under IPC Sections 420, 406, 120B for an alleged property fraud of Rs. 2.3 crore. He has been in custody for 58 days, chargesheet not yet filed. Please provide: (1) Cause title naming State as respondent, (2) Facts of the case in neutral language, (3) Grounds for bail (presumption of innocence, triple test, no flight risk, cooperation, health, delayed chargesheet), (4) Case law: Sanjay Chandra v. CBI, P. Chidambaram, Satender Kumar Antil, Arnab Goswami, (5) Conditions the applicant is willing to accept (surety, passport surrender, reporting), (6) Parity argument if co-accused is on bail, (7) Prayer clause, (8) Verification, (9) List of supporting documents. Format the output as a bail application for sessions court. Consider these constraints: comply with recent Supreme Court directions in Satender Kumar Antil on bail jurisprudence.

prompts.title: Analyze Navtej Singh Johar Section 377 Judgment
prompts.prompt: Act as a constitutional law researcher at the Centre for Law and Policy Research, Bangalore with 10 years experience. I need you to prepare an analytical case brief of Navtej Singh Johar v. Union of India (2018) 10 SCC 1. Here is the context: I am a 3rd year LLB student at Jindal Global Law School writing a paper on constitutional morality for my Law and Society course. Please provide: (1) Full citation, 5-judge bench, and unanimous decision, (2) Background from Naz Foundation, Suresh Kumar Koushal, and the reference in Puttaswamy, (3) Issues framed by the Court, (4) Summary of the four concurring opinions (Misra CJI, Nariman J., Chandrachud J., Malhotra J.), (5) Ratio: partial striking down of Section 377 IPC as violative of Articles 14, 15, 19, 21, (6) Key concepts: constitutional morality v. social morality, transformative constitutionalism, doctrine of progressive realisation, (7) Link to Puttaswamy privacy framework, (8) Impact on subsequent cases like Joseph Shine, Deepika Singh, Supriyo Chakraborty, (9) Legislative silence and unresolved questions on marriage equality, (10) 200-word exam summary. Format the output as an academic case analysis. Consider these constraints: SCC citations, paragraph references, and post-2018 jurisprudence updates.

prompts.title: Draft Franchise Agreement for Indian F&B Business
prompts.prompt: Act as a commercial contracts lawyer with 13 years experience drafting franchise deals for Indian restaurant chains at a Mumbai law firm. I need you to draft a franchise agreement between a franchisor and franchisee for an F&B outlet. Here is the context: I am a 4th year LLB student working on a drafting internship. The franchisor is Masala House Foods Pvt Ltd (Delhi-based) granting a 5-year franchise to Nikhil Agarwal for a restaurant outlet in Jaipur with an initial franchise fee of Rs. 15 lakh and royalty of 6 percent of monthly gross sales. Please provide: (1) Parties and recitals, (2) Grant of franchise clause with territory definition and exclusivity, (3) Term and renewal, (4) Franchise fee, royalty, marketing contribution with payment schedule, (5) Trademark licence and brand standards, (6) Operational standards, training, and quality control, (7) Site selection, fit-out, and opening procedure, (8) Restrictive covenants and non-compete (with Section 27 Contract Act caveat), (9) Termination and post-termination obligations, (10) Dispute resolution via arbitration seated in Delhi, (11) GST and indirect tax allocation, (12) Boilerplate. Format the output as a structured contract. Consider these constraints: Indian Contract Act 1872, Trademarks Act 1999, GST regime, and FSSAI licensing references.

prompts.title: Research Memorandum on Marital Rape Exception
prompts.prompt: Act as a legal researcher with 9 years experience at a women's rights organisation in Delhi. I need you to prepare a research memorandum on the marital rape exception under Indian criminal law. Here is the context: I am a 4th year LLB student writing my seminar paper on criminal law reform at NLU Delhi. Please provide: (1) Historical position: Exception 2 to Section 375 IPC and its BNS 2023 counterpart, (2) Constitutional challenges under Articles 14, 15, 19, 21, (3) Delhi High Court split verdict in RIT Foundation v. Union of India 2022 (Shakdher J. and Hari Shankar J.), (4) Karnataka HC decision in Hrishikesh Sahoo, (5) Pending Supreme Court reference, (6) Justice Verma Committee 2013 recommendation, (7) Law Commission reports, (8) Comparative position: UK (R v. R), Nepal, Australia, South Africa, (9) Arguments for criminalisation (bodily autonomy, Puttaswamy, Joseph Shine), (10) Counter-arguments (family institution, misuse concerns, proviso mechanisms), (11) Proposed legislative amendment text, (12) Bibliography. Format the output as a formal research memorandum. Consider these constraints: Indian context primary, SCC citations, neutral academic tone, and acknowledge both sides.

prompts.title: Prepare Moot Compendium Organization Strategy
prompts.prompt: Act as a moot court coordinator with 11 years experience organising national moot competitions at NLIU Bhopal. I need you to create a complete compendium preparation strategy for a moot team. Here is the context: I am speaker 2 for a team of 3 (two speakers plus researcher) participating in the Surana and Surana National Trial Advocacy Moot in Chennai. The moot proposition is a criminal appeal involving IPC 302, evidentiary issues, and Section 27 Evidence Act. We have 30 days. Please provide: (1) Compendium structure with tabs (statutes, cases, books, articles, miscellaneous), (2) Priority authority list: minimum 15 SC/HC cases with full citations, (3) Tab-wise organisation system with Roman numeral indexing, (4) Highlighting and flagging conventions (pink for ratio, yellow for facts, green for dicta), (5) Pin-point citation spreadsheet template, (6) Day-by-day 30-day preparation calendar, (7) Role division between speakers and researcher, (8) Mock round schedule and feedback loop, (9) Last-mile checklist before submission, (10) Common compendium errors to avoid. Format the output as a team SOP document. Consider these constraints: BCI moot norms, Indian authority preference, and oral round efficiency.

prompts.title: Explain Maneka Gandhi Article 21 Expansion
prompts.prompt: Act as a constitutional law lecturer at Government Law College Mumbai with 19 years teaching experience. I need you to prepare comprehensive study notes on Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India (1978) 1 SCC 248 and the transformation of Article 21. Here is the context: I am a 1st year LLB student preparing for my Constitutional Law I mid-semester and need to understand procedure established by law versus due process. Please provide: (1) Facts involving passport impoundment under Passport Act 1967, (2) Bench composition and lead opinion by Bhagwati J., (3) Issues framed, (4) Ratio: Article 21 procedure must be fair, just, and reasonable, (5) Golden triangle doctrine linking Articles 14, 19, 21, (6) Distinction from A.K. Gopalan and why Gopalan was overruled, (7) Due process importation through the back door, (8) Subsequent expansion: Sunil Batra, Francis Coralie, Olga Tellis, Bandhua Mukti Morcha, Puttaswamy, Common Cause (passive euthanasia), (9) Right to life as right to live with dignity, (10) Exam-ready 350-word answer, (11) 5 possible exam questions. Format the output as structured notes. Consider these constraints: use SCC citations, chronological jurisprudential development, and Indian constitutional focus.

prompts.title: Draft Consumer Complaint under Consumer Protection Act 2019
prompts.prompt: Act as a consumer law advocate with 12 years experience practicing before District and State Consumer Disputes Redressal Commissions in Karnataka. I need you to draft a consumer complaint under the Consumer Protection Act 2019. Here is the context: I am a 3rd year LLB student helping my neighbour Meera Krishnan who purchased a Samsung refrigerator worth Rs. 68,000 from a Croma outlet in Bengaluru that stopped working within 4 months. Despite 6 service visits, the issue persists and the company refuses replacement. Please provide: (1) Cause title with District Commission Bengaluru Urban as forum, (2) Parties (complainant vs. Samsung India and Croma as OP1 and OP2), (3) Jurisdiction clause (territorial and pecuniary under new 2019 thresholds), (4) Facts of the case in chronological paragraphs, (5) Cause of action, (6) Deficiency in service and unfair trade practice grounds, (7) Reliefs sought: replacement, refund, compensation for mental agony, litigation costs, (8) Valuation of claim, (9) Limitation note (2 years under Section 69), (10) Verification and affidavit, (11) List of documents (invoice, service records, emails), (12) Prayer. Format the output as a formal consumer complaint. Consider these constraints: Consumer Protection Act 2019 sections, Rules 2020, and current pecuniary limits (up to Rs. 1 crore district).

prompts.title: Compare IPC 1860 with Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023
prompts.prompt: Act as a criminal law reform expert and former member of the Ministry of Home Affairs committee with 20 years experience. I need you to prepare a detailed comparative analysis between the Indian Penal Code 1860 and the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023. Here is the context: I am a 2nd year LLB student and my Criminal Law II exam will test the transition extensively. Please provide: (1) Structural comparison (511 IPC sections vs 358 BNS sections), (2) Key renumbering table for top 30 offences (murder, theft, cheating, rape, hurt, defamation, etc.), (3) New offences introduced (terrorism, organised crime, mob lynching, snatching), (4) Offences omitted (adultery post-Joseph Shine, Section 377 post-Navtej, sedition transformation into Section 152), (5) Changes in punishments and community service introduction, (6) Gender-neutral provisions, (7) Sedition: Section 124A IPC vs Section 152 BNS (acts endangering sovereignty), (8) Procedural implications with BNSS 2023 and BSA 2023, (9) Critical evaluation: continuity vs change, (10) Transitional provisions and saving clause, (11) Exam preparation tips. Format the output as a structured comparison document with tables. Consider these constraints: official 2023 text, effective 1 July 2024, and accurate section mapping.

prompts.title: Draft Memorandum of Association for Private Limited Company
prompts.prompt: Act as a corporate lawyer with 11 years experience advising company incorporations at a Delhi law firm. I need you to draft a Memorandum of Association for a private limited company under the Companies Act 2013. Here is the context: I am a 3rd year LLB student learning corporate drafting. The proposed company is Aarav EdTech Private Limited with registered office in Gurugram, authorised capital Rs. 10 lakh (1 lakh equity shares of Rs. 10 each), two subscribers (Aarav Sharma and Riya Kapoor), and main business of online test preparation for CLAT and NEET. Please provide: (1) Name clause, (2) Registered office (State clause) naming Haryana, (3) Object clause with main objects, ancillary objects, and other objects (post-2013 amendment format), (4) Liability clause (limited by shares), (5) Capital clause, (6) Association/Subscription clause with subscriber details, (7) Witness clause, (8) Declaration. Format the output as a statutory MOA compliant with Table A of Schedule I of Companies Act 2013. Consider these constraints: strict Companies Act 2013 Section 4 compliance, MCA naming rules, reservation through SPICe+ form, and current Rule 8 of Companies (Incorporation) Rules 2014. Add a 200-word explanatory note on MOA vs AOA for my understanding.

prompts.title: Solve Constitutional Law Hypothetical on Reservations
prompts.prompt: Act as a constitutional law professor and former advocate with 17 years experience in reservation litigation. I need you to solve a constitutional law hypothetical problem. Here is the context: I am a 2nd year LLB student at NLU Bhopal and the exam question asks me to analyse the constitutionality of a state law. The State of Pragatipur enacts a law reserving 75 percent of private sector jobs paying below Rs. 40,000 per month for local domiciles. The law is challenged as violative of Articles 14, 15, 16, and 19(1)(g). Please provide: (1) Identification of constitutional issues, (2) Applicable provisions and their scope, (3) Relevant case law: Indra Sawhney (50 percent ceiling), M.R. Balaji, M. Nagaraj, Jarnail Singh, Janhit Abhiyan (103rd amendment), Dr. Pradeep Jain, (4) Article 16 public employment limitation argument, (5) Article 19(1)(g) business freedom and reasonable restriction analysis, (6) Domicile-based classification and Dr. Pradeep Jain, (7) Haryana 75 percent law Punjab and Haryana HC ruling, (8) Separation of powers consideration, (9) Reasoned conclusion on validity, (10) Model answer structure for a 20-mark question. Format the output as a legal opinion. Consider these constraints: Indian constitutional doctrine, recent 2021-2024 judgments, and federalism dimension.

prompts.title: Draft Partnership Deed under Indian Partnership Act
prompts.prompt: Act as a commercial lawyer with 14 years experience drafting partnership and LLP documents in Ahmedabad. I need you to draft a partnership deed under the Indian Partnership Act 1932. Here is the context: I am a 2nd year LLB student at Gujarat National Law University and my drafting assignment requires a deed for a three-partner textile trading firm in Surat. Partners are Hiren Patel, Dhruv Shah, and Meet Desai with capital contributions of Rs. 15 lakh, Rs. 10 lakh, and Rs. 10 lakh respectively, profit-sharing ratio 45:30:25, and firm name Triveni Textiles. Please provide: (1) Date, parties, and recitals, (2) Firm name and place of business, (3) Nature of business and commencement date, (4) Capital contribution and interest on capital, (5) Profit and loss sharing ratio, (6) Duties and powers of each partner, (7) Bank accounts and operation, (8) Books of accounts and financial year, (9) Admission, retirement, and expulsion of partner, (10) Dissolution clauses, (11) Arbitration under 1996 Act, (12) Execution and witnessing, (13) Stamp and registration note. Format the output as a formal partnership deed. Consider these constraints: Indian Partnership Act 1932 Sections 4-69, registration under Section 58-59, Gujarat stamp duty, and GST implications.

prompts.title: Analyze Shayara Bano Triple Talaq Judgment
prompts.prompt: Act as a personal law professor at Jamia Millia Islamia with 15 years teaching experience in Muslim personal law. I need you to produce a detailed case analysis of Shayara Bano v. Union of India (2017) 9 SCC 1. Here is the context: I am a 3rd year LLB student preparing for my Family Law II exam focused on Muslim personal law reforms. Please provide: (1) Citation and 5-judge multi-faith bench composition, (2) Facts involving instant talaq-e-biddat, (3) Issues framed, (4) The 3:2 split with separate opinions of Khehar CJI, Nariman J., Lalit J., Kurian J., Nazeer J., (5) Ratio: talaq-e-biddat struck down as unconstitutional, (6) Majority reasoning on Article 14 and manifest arbitrariness doctrine, (7) Minority view on protection of personal laws, (8) Relationship to Articles 25, 26, 44, (9) Muslim Women (Protection of Rights on Marriage) Act 2019 enactment, (10) Impact on nikah halala and polygamy pending cases, (11) Critical commentary on judicial intervention in personal laws, (12) 200-word exam summary. Format the output as a case analysis. Consider these constraints: SCC citations, neutral academic tone, and reference to Sabarimala case dialogue on personal laws.

prompts.title: Draft Public Interest Litigation Petition
prompts.prompt: Act as a public interest litigation advocate with 20 years experience filing PILs in the Supreme Court and Delhi High Court. I need you to draft a PIL under Article 32 of the Constitution. Here is the context: I am a 4th year LLB student at NLU Delhi drafting this for my Environmental Law course. The issue is untreated sewage discharge into the Yamuna river from three drains near Okhla affecting the right to clean water and Article 21 of residents downstream. Please provide: (1) Cause title in Supreme Court, (2) Petitioner as a registered environmental NGO with locus standi paragraph citing S.P. Gupta, (3) Jurisdiction under Article 32, (4) Facts with scientific data and photographs reference, (5) Legal grounds: Article 21 right to clean environment (Subhash Kumar, M.C. Mehta), Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act 1974, Environment Protection Act 1986, National Green Tribunal Act 2010, (6) Violation of precautionary principle and polluter pays, (7) Reliefs: mandamus to DPCC, CPCB, DJB, and MoEFCC, monitoring committee, compensation, (8) Interim reliefs, (9) Verification, (10) Annexures. Format the output as a formal writ petition. Consider these constraints: Article 32 standards, PIL discipline, avoid frivolous grounds, and Supreme Court Rules 2013 compliance.

prompts.title: Explain Doctrine of Pith and Substance with Examples
prompts.prompt: Act as a federalism and constitutional law professor at NLU Odisha with 13 years teaching experience. I need you to prepare study notes on the doctrine of pith and substance as used in Indian federalism. Here is the context: I am a 2nd year LLB student preparing for my Constitutional Law II exam covering Part XI of the Constitution and the Seventh Schedule. Please provide: (1) Meaning and purpose of the doctrine, (2) Article 246 and the three Lists (Union, State, Concurrent), (3) Historical origin and adoption in Indian context, (4) Landmark cases: State of Bombay v. F.N. Balsara, Prafulla Kumar Mukherjee v. Bank of Commerce, Kartar Singh v. State of Punjab, Union of India v. Shah Goverdhan L. Kabra Teachers' College, (5) Test: true nature and character of legislation, not form, (6) Incidental encroachment tolerance, (7) Application in GST Council challenges and Mohit Minerals case 2022, (8) Relationship with colourable legislation doctrine and ultra vires, (9) Five hypothetical fact patterns with application, (10) Exam answer template for 15 marks. Format the output as structured study notes. Consider these constraints: Indian federal scheme, SCC citations, and concrete examples from Indian legislation.

prompts.title: Draft Divorce Petition under Hindu Marriage Act
prompts.prompt: Act as a family law advocate with 18 years practice before the Family Courts in Bengaluru. I need you to draft a divorce petition under Section 13(1) of the Hindu Marriage Act 1955. Here is the context: I am a 3rd year LLB student doing a family law drafting assignment. The petitioner is Anjali Reddy (wife) seeking divorce from Respondent Karthik Reddy on grounds of cruelty and desertion. They married on 18 November 2019 at Bengaluru, have no children, and have been living separately since March 2023. Please provide: (1) Cause title before Principal Family Court Bengaluru, (2) Parties and their addresses, (3) Jurisdiction clause citing Section 19 HMA, (4) Facts of marriage and breakdown in chronological paragraphs, (5) Specific instances of mental and physical cruelty, (6) Desertion particulars meeting 2-year requirement, (7) Grounds for divorce under Section 13(1)(i-a) and (i-b), (8) Absence of condonation, collusion, connivance clause, (9) Relief: decree of divorce, permanent alimony under Section 25, return of streedhan, (10) Verification, (11) List of documents (marriage certificate, photos, correspondence). Format the output as a formal petition. Consider these constraints: HMA 1955 compliance, Family Courts Act 1984 procedure, Naveen Kohli and Samar Ghosh cruelty standards, and Karnataka court practice.

prompts.title: Prepare Evidence Act Study Notes on Section 27
prompts.prompt: Act as an evidence law professor with 16 years experience teaching at ILS Law College Pune. I need you to prepare detailed study notes on Section 27 of the Indian Evidence Act 1872 (corresponding Section 23 of Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam 2023). Here is the context: I am a 3rd year LLB student preparing for Evidence Law semester exam and this section is tested every year. Please provide: (1) Text of Section 27 and plain language explanation, (2) Interplay with Sections 25 and 26 (confessions to police), (3) Essential conditions: information from accused in custody, discovery of a fact, distinct portion admissible, (4) Landmark cases: Pulukuri Kottaya v. Emperor (1947), State of Maharashtra v. Damu, Mohmed Inayatullah, Aghnoo Nagesia, Anter Singh, (5) Discovery must be a fact not already known, (6) Joint disclosures and recovery jurisprudence, (7) Section 27 v. Article 20(3) self-incrimination, (8) Relationship with Section 8 (conduct), (9) Common exam traps and how to avoid, (10) Five hypothetical problems with model answers, (11) BSA 2023 Section 23 changes if any. Format the output as exam-ready notes. Consider these constraints: Indian jurisprudence, SCC citations, and Pulukuri Kottaya as foundational authority.

prompts.title: Draft Power of Attorney for Property Management
prompts.prompt: Act as a property lawyer with 15 years experience drafting conveyancing documents in Kolkata. I need you to draft a special power of attorney for property management. Here is the context: I am a 2nd year LLB student at NUJS Kolkata preparing a drafting exercise. The principal is Dr. Arundhati Banerjee (resident of London) authorising her brother Subhashish Banerjee (resident of Salt Lake Kolkata) to manage her ancestral flat in Ballygunge. Please provide: (1) Parties and recitals identifying principal and agent, (2) Description of the property, (3) Specific powers granted: rent collection, tenant management, repairs, payment of taxes and society dues, representing before authorities, bank operations related to rent, filing tax returns, (4) Powers expressly NOT granted (no sale, no mortgage, no gift), (5) Duration and revocation clause, (6) Indemnity to principal, (7) Ratification clause, (8) Governing law (Indian) and West Bengal jurisdiction, (9) Execution formalities, (10) Notarisation/consular attestation note for cross-border validity, (11) Registration requirement under Registration Act 1908. Format the output as a formal POA. Consider these constraints: Powers of Attorney Act 1882, Registration Act, Suraj Lamp guidelines against GPA sales, Hague Apostille Convention applicability, and West Bengal stamp duty.

prompts.title: Solve Contract Law Problem on Offer and Acceptance
prompts.prompt: Act as a contract law tutor at Amity Law School Noida with 12 years experience. I need you to solve a contract law problem under the Indian Contract Act 1872. Here is the context: I am a 1st year LLB student and the tutorial question asks me to apply offer, acceptance, and communication rules. On 1 April 2026, Vikram posts a letter from Jaipur offering to sell his vintage Royal Enfield motorcycle to Suresh in Pune for Rs. 2.5 lakh, to be accepted by 10 April. On 5 April Suresh posts his acceptance. On 6 April Vikram sends a revocation email. On 7 April Suresh receives the revocation email. On 8 April Vikram receives the acceptance letter. Is there a binding contract? Please provide: (1) Identification of legal issues, (2) Applicable sections 3, 4, 5, 7 of the Contract Act, (3) Rule in Adams v. Lindsell adopted in India via Bhagwandas Goverdhandas Kedia v. Girdharilal, (4) When offer can be revoked (Section 5), (5) Moment of complete communication for acceptance (Section 4), (6) Step by step timeline analysis, (7) Conclusion on contract formation, (8) What if the medium was instantaneous (email, phone), (9) Model answer framework, (10) Three variant scenarios and their outcomes. Format the output as a legal opinion. Consider these constraints: Indian Contract Act, Indian and English authorities, and clear IRAC structure.

prompts.title: Research Anti-Defection Law under Tenth Schedule
prompts.prompt: Act as a constitutional law researcher at the Vidhi Centre for Legal Policy with 9 years experience. I need you to prepare a research note on the anti-defection law under the Tenth Schedule of the Constitution of India. Here is the context: I am a 4th year LLB student writing a term paper on parliamentary disqualification for my Advanced Constitutional Law course. Please provide: (1) Historical background and the 52nd Amendment 1985, (2) Text and structure of the Tenth Schedule, (3) Grounds of disqualification (voluntary giving up membership, voting against party whip), (4) Exceptions: merger of two-thirds members, (5) Role of the Speaker/Chairman as tribunal, (6) Landmark cases: Kihoto Hollohan v. Zachillhu, Ravi S. Naik, Rajendra Singh Rana, Keisham Meghachandra, Shrimanth Balasaheb Patil, Subhash Desai v. Governor Maharashtra 2023, (7) Judicial review of Speaker's decisions, (8) Time-limit controversy and 3-month guideline, (9) 91st Amendment 2003 removal of split exception, (10) Recent crises: Karnataka, Madhya Pradesh, Maharashtra, Goa, (11) Reform suggestions (independent tribunal, Dinesh Goswami Committee), (12) Bibliography. Format the output as an academic research note. Consider these constraints: SCC citations, neutral analysis, and acknowledge 2023 Constitution Bench dicta.

prompts.title: Draft Rent Agreement under Maharashtra Rent Control Act
prompts.prompt: Act as a property lawyer with 13 years experience drafting residential leases in Mumbai and Thane. I need you to draft a leave and licence agreement under the Maharashtra Rent Control Act 1999. Here is the context: I am a 2nd year LLB student at GLC Mumbai working on a drafting assignment. Licensor is Kiran Shetty owning a 2BHK flat in Andheri West, Licensee is Pooja Menon (working professional at an MNC), monthly licence fee Rs. 55,000, security deposit Rs. 3.3 lakh, 11-month term commencing 1 May 2026. Please provide: (1) Parties and recitals, (2) Description of premises with society details, (3) Term of 11 months (to avoid Rent Act protection), (4) Licence fee and payment mode (bank transfer only), (5) Security deposit and refund terms, (6) Permitted use (residential, single family), (7) Maintenance and society charges responsibility, (8) Utilities (electricity, water, internet) allocation, (9) Restrictions (no subletting, no pets subject to society rules, no alteration), (10) Termination with 2-month notice, (11) Inventory schedule, (12) Registration mandatory under Section 55 Maharashtra Rent Control Act, (13) Stamp duty as per Schedule I Maharashtra Stamp Act. Format the output as a formal leave and licence agreement. Consider these constraints: strict compliance with Section 55 MRC Act, compulsory registration, and avoidance of tenancy creation.

prompts.title: Prepare Constitutional Law Oral Argument Outline
prompts.prompt: Act as a senior advocate practicing before the Supreme Court of India with 25 years experience in constitutional matters. I need you to prepare a complete oral argument outline for a moot court competition. Here is the context: I am lead counsel for the Petitioner in the NLS Trilegal Moot 2026 challenging a hypothetical state law that mandates digital ID for accessing ration shops, challenged under Articles 14, 21, and the Puttaswamy proportionality test. We have 15 minutes of oral time. Please provide: (1) 60-second opening with case theory, (2) Three-issue breakdown with 4 minutes each, (3) For each issue: principle, application, authority, illustration (PAAI framework), (4) Key authorities with pin-point citations: Puttaswamy I and II, Aadhaar (K.S. Puttaswamy II), Anuradha Bhasin, Modern Dental College, (5) Three-part proportionality test application, (6) Anticipated bench questions and model responses, (7) Rebuttal reservations, (8) 30-second closing prayer, (9) Speaking rate calculation (140 wpm target), (10) Notes on courtroom etiquette and bench management. Format the output as a speaker notes document. Consider these constraints: strict 15-minute budget, Indian constitutional framework, BCI moot etiquette, and realistic judge question anticipation.

prompts.title: Explain Judicial Review in India with Key Cases
prompts.prompt: Act as a constitutional law professor at NLU Delhi with 18 years teaching experience. I need you to prepare comprehensive study notes on judicial review in India. Here is the context: I am a 2nd year LLB student and this topic is a 20-mark question in my Constitutional Law II exam focusing on separation of powers. Please provide: (1) Definition and sources (Articles 13, 32, 131, 132, 136, 226, 227), (2) Scope: legislative, executive, and constitutional amendments, (3) Basic structure doctrine and judicial review as part of it (L. Chandra Kumar, Minerva Mills), (4) Grounds of review: illegality, irrationality, procedural impropriety, proportionality (Associated Provincial Picture Houses v. Wednesbury, Om Kumar, Modern Dental College), (5) Limits: political questions, policy matters, Rajasthan v. Union, S.R. Bommai, (6) Doctrine of prospective overruling (I.C. Golaknath, India Cement), (7) Curative petitions (Rupa Ashok Hurra), (8) Judicial review of ordinances (Krishna Kumar Singh), (9) Comparison with US constitutional practice in 100 words, (10) Critique: judicial overreach debate, (11) Exam-ready answer framework. Format the output as structured exam notes. Consider these constraints: Indian authorities primary, SCC citations, and balanced treatment of review limits.

prompts.title: Draft Arbitration Clause and Standalone Agreement
prompts.prompt: Act as an arbitration lawyer with 16 years experience handling institutional and ad-hoc arbitrations in Delhi and Mumbai. I need you to draft both a model arbitration clause and a standalone arbitration agreement under the Arbitration and Conciliation Act 1996. Here is the context: I am a 4th year LLB student taking an Alternative Dispute Resolution elective at NALSAR Hyderabad. The underlying dispute is a B2B commercial supply contract between Mahindra Logistics (consignor) and Bharat Traders (consignee) for pan-India transport with annual contract value Rs. 12 crore. Please provide: (1) Part A: a 150-word model clause to insert in the main contract, (2) Part B: a full standalone arbitration agreement, (3) Number of arbitrators (sole for disputes under Rs. 50 lakh, 3-member panel above), (4) Appointment mechanism with fallback to Delhi International Arbitration Centre, (5) Seat (Delhi) and venue, (6) Language (English) and governing law (Indian), (7) Rules: DIAC Rules 2023 or UNCITRAL, (8) Interim measures under Section 9, (9) Fast track option under Section 29B if applicable, (10) Confidentiality under Section 42A, (11) Finality and challenge under Section 34. Format the output as a clean agreement. Consider these constraints: 2015, 2019, and 2021 amendments, BALCO seat jurisprudence, and PASL Wind Solutions guidance.
